Row1.Rationale: METΔex14 removes the CBL-binding Y1003 site, impairing receptor degradation and prolonging MET-driven PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK signaling; crizotinib, a type Ia MET TKI, inhibits MET autophosphorylation and reduces proliferation in METΔex14-driven models and patients, producing clinical benefit but generally suboptimal durability compared with more selective MET TKIs.

Row2.Rationale: Acquired MET D1228N/V/H/E and Y1230C/H/S/D/N mutations in METex14 NSCLC alter the ATP-binding pocket and activation loop, weaken interactions with type I MET TKIs including crizotinib, and are repeatedly observed at progression, restoring MET signaling despite drug exposure; these mutants remain more sensitive to type II MET inhibitors.

Row3.Rationale: The G1163R solvent-front mutation disrupts the key contact used by type Ia MET inhibitors; in METex14 tumors it selectively mediates resistance to crizotinib while sparing type Ib and type II MET TKIs, enabling continued MET-driven downstream signaling under crizotinib.

Row4.Rationale: METΔex14 tumors show preferential KRAS/MAPK activation, and co-occurring KRAS mutations or amplification, as well as NF1 loss, drive constitutive ERK signaling that bypasses MET blockade; in Ba/F3 and patient-derived models, KRAS activation shifts crizotinib IC50 upward and maintains pERK, while adding MEK inhibitor trametinib to crizotinib restores apoptosis and drug sensitivity.

Row5.Rationale: METΔex14 enhances PI3K/AKT signaling via prolonged receptor activation; crizotinib suppresses MET phosphorylation and reduces pAKT in MET-driven models, contributing to tumor regression, but PI3K/AKT can be reactivated via KRAS/MAPK or RTK bypass, limiting depth and duration of response.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: ERBB family (EGFR/HER2/HER3) bypass in MET-driven disease
Row6.Rationale: In METex14-positive NSCLC, off-target resistance to type I MET TKIs arises via amplification or activation of EGFR, ERBB2, and ERBB3, which can heterodimerize and reactivate MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling independently of MET; combined MET plus EGFR/ERBB inhibition is required to fully suppress downstream pathways in preclinical models.

Row7.Rationale: Selective amplification of the METΔex14 allele increases receptor density and signaling output, which can outstrip achievable crizotinib exposure; METamp is enriched at acquired resistance and can coexist with METΔex14, supporting persistent MET addiction despite TKI therapy.

Row8.Rationale: MET activation recruits STAT3, promoting transcription of survival genes; in METΔex14 models, prolonged receptor signaling sustains STAT3 activation, which may contribute to cell persistence under MET inhibition and interact with MAPK/PI3K pathways to blunt crizotinib-induced apoptosis.

Row9.Rationale: ROS1 fusions constitutively activate SHP2–RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathways; crizotinib potently inhibits ROS1 kinase, producing high response rates and median PFS ~19 months in ROS1-rearranged NSCLC, with marked tumor shrinkage until secondary resistance develops.

Row10.Pathway ID/Name: ROS1 kinase domain solvent-front/gatekeeper (G2032R, D2033N, L2026M)
Row10.Rationale: Solvent-front mutations G2032R and D2033N and gatekeeper L2026M remodel the ATP pocket, creating steric hindrance or altered hydrophobicity that markedly reduces crizotinib affinity while preserving ROS1 signaling; G2032R accounts for ~35–40% of post-crizotinib ROS1 resistance and confers high-level cross-resistance to several first/second-generation ROS1 TKIs.

Row11.Rationale: In ROS1-rearranged lung cancer progressing on crizotinib, activation of EGFR or HER2 establishes alternative RTK inputs that reactivate JAK/STAT3, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, and MAPK signaling, reducing dependence on ROS1; HER2-mediated bypass and EGFR activation are documented off-target resistance mechanisms necessitating combined RTK or downstream pathway blockade.

Row12.Rationale: In ROS1 fusion-positive patients relapsing on crizotinib or lorlatinib, acquired KRAS G12C mutations, KRAS amplification, NRAS mutation, and MAP2K1 mutation are detected and correlate with ROS1-independent MAPK reactivation; these lesions sustain ERK signaling despite ROS1 inhibition, suggesting that combining ROS1 TKIs with MEK/ERK pathway inhibitors may overcome resistance.
